en.title: IELTS writing①
en.prompt:
Using the IELTS Writing Task scoring criteria, please respond to the following theme four times, each with a different level of proficiency: 9, 8, 7, and 6.

The theme is: 'Rich countries often give money to poorer countries, but it does not solve poverty. Therefore, developed countries should give other types of help to the poor countries rather than financial aid. To what extent do you agree or disagree? Your essay should be at least 250 words.'

For each response, please begin with the title 'Response for Score X', where X is the score level (9, 8, 7, or 6).

After the four responses, explain why each response deserves its respective score, considering the following aspects: task achievement, coherence and cohesion, lexical resource, and grammatical range and accuracy. Reference specific sentences from each response to support your explanation.
en.remark: Compose IELTS essays on the same topic with varying scores, accompanied by the reasons for the ratings. Contributed by @fansi2020.

related.title: High schoolers' essay
related.remark: The article produced by this prompt reflects the writing style of Chinese middle school students. Contributed by @Qizhen-Yang.

related.title: Four-layered Structure Induction
related.remark: Multi-level summarization and induction can be used to explain words and phrases and make associations with the article. The Chinese version of this prompt has better effect. Contributed by @ergf991.

metaDescription: Preparing for IELTS? Generate essays scoring 6-9 on specific topics. Analyze why each score was given based on criteria.

faq.q: Is the AI's scoring consistent with real examiners?
faq.a: The scoring dimensions (Task Response / Coherence / Lexical / Grammar) framework is accurate, but specific scores are sometimes 0.5 too lenient or strict. Fine as self-practice reference. Real official scoring requires a mock exam or feedback from a foreign teacher.

faq.q: What's the difference from ID 249 IELTS writing?
faq.a: Both prompts are two versions from the same author with slight config differences. Try both once, see which has clearer scoring logic and example essays closer to examiner taste, and pick one to stick with. Switching back and forth hinders focused learning.
